currentUse: Church site at 331 Reed Street, Pennsport, Philadelphia — long a vacant lot — has been redeveloped as a new-construction luxury townhouse ("The Enclaves" development), listed at $1,100,000 with a 2026 build year. The parish cemetery in Bensalem PA survives but is abandoned, with township/volunteer upkeep.
summary: St. Mary's Lithuanian National Catholic Church stood at 331 Reed Street in Philadelphia's Pennsport neighborhood; the building is long gone and the address was documented as a vacant lot by both Global True Lithuania and the Lithuanian Genealogy cemetery database. As of July 2026 that vacancy has ended: 331 Reed St (19147) is an active listing for a new-construction 3-bed/3-bath townhouse in "The Enclaves" development, priced at $1,100,000 with a 2026 build year and expected Q3 2026 delivery — the former church footprint is now upscale residential real estate. The parish itself was organized in 1926 and, per Global True Lithuania, functioned roughly 1926-1957 with about 400 members at its start. Its most tangible surviving legacy is St. Mary's Lithuanian National Catholic Cemetery on Galloway Road in Bensalem, Bucks County — abandoned, repeatedly vandalized, and heavily overgrown, but kept minimally alive since 1993 by municipal worker Chris Schrier (who won $500/year in township landscaping funds) with support from Rev. Peter Burkauskas of Philadelphia's St. Andrew's Lithuanian (Roman Catholic) parish. Cemetery ownership remains murky — a 2000 Baltimore Sun investigation suggested title may rest with the Polish National Catholic Church in Scranton — and as of 2015 the township was still seeking a Lithuanian organization to assume upkeep, with the Lithuanian American Community of Philadelphia having expressed interest after storm damage toppled trees onto tombstones. Only about 22 graves are recorded in the genealogy database.

developments.headline: Baltimore Sun documents the abandoned Bensalem cemetery and its volunteer caretaker
developments.detail: The Sun profiled Chris Schrier, a Bensalem municipal worker who discovered the overgrown St. Mary's Lithuanian National Catholic Cemetery in 1993 and maintained it for seven years, partnering with Rev. Peter Burkauskas of Philadelphia's St. Andrew's Lithuanian RC parish. The article records decades of vandalism (toppled headstones, destroyed porcelain funeral portraits), $500/year township landscaping funding Schrier secured, and unclear ownership records possibly pointing to the Polish National Catholic Church in Scranton.

sources.title: Pair tracks last resting place of lost sect

developments.headline: Township seeks a Lithuanian organization to take over the cemetery; Lithuanian American Community of Philadelphia expresses interest
developments.detail: The Lithuanian Genealogy cemetery database records that Bensalem Township cuts the grass in summer (via a good samaritan), places flowers and a Christmas wreath, and is looking for a Lithuanian church or organization to assume upkeep. As of February 2015, after storm damage caused fallen trees to harm tombstones, the Lithuanian American Community of Philadelphia had expressed interest in assuming ownership. The database records 22 graves.

sources.title: St. Mary's Lithuanian National Catholic Cemetery: Bensalem, PA

developments.headline: Former church lot at 331 Reed Street redeveloped as a $1.1M new-construction townhouse
developments.detail: The address documented by Lithuanian heritage sources as the church's site (and later a vacant lot) is now an active listing: a 3-bed/3-bath, 2,864 sq ft townhouse in 'The Enclaves' new-construction community in Pennsport, built 2026, priced at $1,100,000 with a 10-year tax abatement and expected Q3 2026 delivery. The listing had been online 247 days as of July 16, 2026 (i.e., listed around late 2025).

sources.title: St. Mary's Lithuanian National Catholic Cemetery: Bensalem, PA
Pair tracks last resting place of lost sect

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Three refinements/tensions with the archive record. (1) Schism date: the archive says the parish broke from Rome in the 1916 schism, but the Baltimore Sun (2000) describes the Lithuanian National Catholic sect as having broken from Rome in 1902, and both Global True Lithuania and LithuanianGenealogy.org date the Philadelphia parish's organization to 1926 — a decade later than the archive's schism framing implies for this specific congregation. (2) Closure date: the archive gives no closure date but implies operation until a 1972 building sale with ~30-40 parishioners; Global True Lithuania states the parish existed 1926-1957, though it also says Philadelphia's Lithuanian National Catholics 'vanished in the 1970s' — these may be reconcilable (parish life ending late 1950s, property disposed of by 1972), but no opened source independently confirms the 1972 sale or a sale price. (3) The archive's claim that the Bensalem cemetery survives is confirmed, with the refinement that it is abandoned, vandalized over decades, and maintained only minimally by township-funded volunteers, with ownership possibly resting with the Polish National Catholic Church in Scranton rather than any Lithuanian body. The demolition itself is corroborated: two independent sources confirm 331 Reed Street was long a vacant lot, and it is now new residential construction.
gaps: No opened source confirms the archive's 1972 sale year, a sale price, or a buyer for the church property; deed-level Philadelphia property records were not searched. The exact demolition year is unrecorded in any source found. Founding/closure dates conflict across sources (1902 vs 1916 schism; 1926 organization; 1957 vs 1970s ending) and could not be adjudicated. A September 2023 YouTube video ('Saint Mary's Cemetery In Bensalem PA - Rejuvenation Efforts') surfaced in search results suggesting recent volunteer cleanup activity, but the video page could not be fetched to verify its content, and Find a Grave (cemetery #2252891) returned HTTP 403, so current memorial counts and 2023-2026 cemetery condition are unverified. Whether the Lithuanian American Community of Philadelphia ever completed the ownership transfer it explored in 2015 is unknown. No diocesan involvement exists to track, since the parish was never diocesan — consistent with the archive.
